repositoryPolicy.intendedUse: Offline patient education, care preparation, record organization, and safety-boundary support.
repositoryPolicy.copyrightedTextPolicy: Do not store copyrighted textbooks, full guidelines, or proprietary protocols unless licensing allows it.
repositoryPolicy.productionGate: Every imported clinical source requires source approval, license review, PHI removal, clinician review, validation tests, and rollback metadata.

sourceRegistry.name: Public Health Guideline Pack
sourceRegistry.licensePolicy: Use only open, public-domain, or organization-approved licensed content.
sourceRegistry.verificationGate: source approval + clinician review + safety red-team before production use

sourceRegistry.name: Clinical Reference Pack
sourceRegistry.licensePolicy: Do not commit copyrighted full text. Store approved abstracts, metadata, and clinician-reviewed summaries only.
sourceRegistry.verificationGate: license review + specialty review + rollback-ready release

sourceRegistry.name: Medicine Reference Pack
sourceRegistry.licensePolicy: Store normalized medicine-name metadata and safety summaries, not prescribing decisions.

sourceRegistry.name: Lab and Imaging Reference Pack
sourceRegistry.licensePolicy: Store plain-language explanation and code metadata; do not interpret raw images as diagnosis.

sourceRegistry.name: Healthcare Operations Pack
sourceRegistry.licensePolicy: Store workflow checklists and non-PHI templates only.
sourceRegistry.verificationGate: privacy review + document redaction + audit logging

sourcePacks.name: Chronic and Longitudinal Care Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: Expanded local offline records for chronic disease monitoring, home follow-up, specialist review, longitudinal safety boundaries, and day-to-day care preparation.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and safety review before production medical use

sourcePacks.name: Clinical Guidance Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: Supplemental local clinical guidance topics for common specialist, safety, and symptom-review pathways.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician review before production medical use

sourcePacks.name: Comprehensive Local Care Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: Supplemental local coverage for home vitals, specialist follow-up, medication safety, lab interpretation, wellness planning, records, insurance readiness, and procedures.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and workflow review before production medical use

sourcePacks.name: Edge-Case Safety Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: High-risk mixed-signal records for chest symptoms, low glucose, medicine reactions, child fever, stroke-like headache, palpitations, dehydration, and repeat blood-pressure checks.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and safety review before production medical use

sourcePacks.name: High-Frequency Offline RAG Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: High-frequency symptom, medicine, hydration, respiratory, digestive, urinary, pain, and home-monitoring records tuned for stronger offline general and medicine retrieval.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and safety review before production medical use

sourcePacks.name: Operations and Prevention Expansion Pack
sourcePacks.description: Supplemental local records, insurance, visit, prevention, and follow-up workflow knowledge.
sourcePacks.reviewStatus: local pack requires workflow and privacy review before production deployment

whatToTrack: Exactly what warning sign is happening now, when it started, and whether it is worsening.

records.safetyNotes: Chest pain, severe headache, new weakness, breathing trouble, confusion, or very high readings with symptoms need urgent real-world medical review.
aliases: Hypertension Home Trend and Symptom Review
whatToTrack: At least two or three seated home readings with time, symptoms, and medicine timing.
Salt intake, stress, caffeine, hydration, exercise, and sleep changes around higher readings.
Any headache, vision change, dizziness, chest discomfort, or breathlessness.
Symptom pattern, duration, progression, and the effect on routine activities or sleep.
Relevant readings, report values, medicine timing, and previous diagnosis context.
careQuestions: Does the trend suggest a stable pattern, poor technique, or a need for sooner clinician review?
Which symptom and reading combinations should move this from routine monitoring to urgent assessment?

precautions: Do not change blood-pressure medicine doses from app guidance alone.
Do not rely on one isolated reading when symptoms or repeat values suggest a higher-risk pattern.
Chest pain, severe headache, new weakness, breathing trouble, confusion, or very high readings with symptoms need urgent real-world medical review.

records.title: Anxiety, Sleep, and Daily Function Support Review
records.summary: Builds a structured anxiety review using triggers, sleep disruption, concentration, body symptoms, support systems, coping routine, and safety boundaries for worsening distress.
records.safetyNotes: Self-harm thoughts, inability to stay safe, severe confusion, chest pain, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms need urgent real-world help.
aliases: Anxiety, Sleep, and Daily Function Support Review
whatToTrack: Trigger pattern, body symptoms, sleep disruption, appetite, and concentration changes.
What support is available now, whether the person can function safely, and what coping steps help.
Any chest symptoms, fainting, suicidal thoughts, or rapidly worsening distress.
Age, risk factors, family history, chronic conditions, vaccines, and last screening dates.
Diet, sleep, hydration, activity, stress, tobacco, and alcohol patterns that affect risk.
careQuestions: What makes this a routine support conversation versus a same-day safety issue?
Which function and sleep details should be shared during follow-up?
Which prevention goals are most relevant for Anxiety, Sleep, and Daily Function Support Review?
What screening, vaccine, or habit questions should be reviewed next?
How can progress be tracked safely between routine care visits?
precautions: Do not dismiss dangerous thoughts or major functional collapse as routine anxiety.
Do not let mental-wellness framing hide chest pain, fainting, or breathing warning signs.
Self-harm thoughts, inability to stay safe, severe confusion, chest pain, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms need urgent real-world help.
Prevention guidance supports routine care planning and does not replace clinician review.
New or concerning symptoms should be handled as active medical issues, not prevention only.

records.title: Blood Thinner, Bruising, and Bleeding Safety Review
records.summary: Organizes blood-thinner safety questions using bruising, bleeding, black stool, falls, missed doses, procedure timing, new medicines, and pharmacist or clinician follow-up needs.
records.safetyNotes: Head injury, major bleeding, black stool, vomiting blood, fainting, severe weakness, or breathing trouble needs urgent real-world medical care.
aliases: Blood Thinner, Bruising, and Bleeding Safety Review
whatToTrack: Exact medicine name, recent bruising or bleeding pattern, falls, and missed or late doses.
Any new medicines, pain relievers, alcohol changes, planned procedures, or black stool.
Head injury, dizziness, severe weakness, or shortness of breath.
Medicine name, strength, form, route, timing, and the reason it is being taken.
Recent missed doses, side effects, new over-the-counter products, supplements, or alcohol use.
careQuestions: Which bleeding patterns can wait for a pharmacist or clinician call and which need urgent care?
What details should be ready before a blood-thinner follow-up or procedure discussion?
How should the label, warnings, and common questions for Blood Thinner, Bruising, and Bleeding Safety Review be reviewed?
Which interaction, storage, or side-effect details should be confirmed with a pharmacist?
What symptoms would make a medicine concern urgent instead of routine?
precautions: Do not decide blood-thinner dose changes from this app.
Do not ignore falls, head injury, or black stool.
Head injury, major bleeding, black stool, vomiting blood, fainting, severe weakness, or breathing trouble needs urgent real-world medical care.
The local library does not prescribe, approve, or calculate medicine doses.
Do not double a missed dose unless a licensed clinician or pharmacist instructed it.

records.title: HbA1c Trend, Meals, and Home Log Review
records.summary: Connects HbA1c review with meal timing, fasting and post-meal readings, illness, activity, medicines, and follow-up planning for diabetes care.
records.safetyNotes: Confusion, repeated vomiting, very high sugar symptoms, or very low sugar symptoms should be handled with prompt real-world review.
aliases: HbA1c Trend, Meals, and Home Log Review
whatToTrack: Current HbA1c, fasting readings, post-meal readings, and how they compare with older results.
Meal pattern, activity, hydration, illness, and medicine timing around higher readings.
Any weight change, foot issues, sleep changes, or glucose symptoms.
The exact test name, result value, units, reference range, date, and whether the test was fasting.
Symptoms present at the time of the test and whether this is a new or repeated result.
careQuestions: Which log details make the diabetes follow-up more actionable than a single HbA1c value alone?
What additional home patterns should be reviewed before the next visit?
What does HbA1c Trend, Meals, and Home Log Review mean in plain language and what context matters most?

What symptoms or companion results would make this lab concern more urgent?
precautions: Do not use one HbA1c number alone to change medicines without clinician guidance.
Do not ignore symptoms just because the lab follow-up is already scheduled.
Confusion, repeated vomiting, very high sugar symptoms, or very low sugar symptoms should be handled with prompt real-world review.
Lab results need patient context, trends, and clinician review before decisions are made.
Do not diagnose or change treatment from a single report value alone.

aliases: Vaccine Reaction, Fever, and Allergy Boundary Review
whatToTrack: When the reaction started, which vaccine was given, and whether the symptoms are local or whole body.

Whether symptoms are improving over hours, staying the same, or worsening.
Age, risk factors, family history, chronic conditions, vaccines, and last screening dates.

records.title: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Dehydration, and Kidney-Medicine Review
records.summary: Connects vomiting or diarrhea with dehydration, urine output, dizziness, blood-pressure or diabetes medicines, and the need for pharmacist or clinician review when the person cannot keep up with fluids.
records.safetyNotes: Repeated vomiting, confusion, fainting, reduced urine, severe weakness, or worsening illness needs prompt medical review.
aliases: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Dehydration, and Kidney-Medicine Review
whatToTrack: Vomiting or diarrhea frequency, ability to drink, urine output, dizziness, and any blood-pressure or glucose readings.
Which medicines were taken recently, especially kidney-, blood-pressure-, or diabetes-related medicines.
Weakness, confusion, fainting, fever, and whether the illness is improving or worsening.
Medicine name, strength, form, route, timing, and the reason it is being taken.
Recent missed doses, side effects, new over-the-counter products, supplements, or alcohol use.
careQuestions: Which dehydration signs mean medicine questions should move from routine review to prompt clinician or pharmacist review?
What urine, fluid, and medicine-timing details matter most for the next follow-up?
How should the label, warnings, and common questions for Vomiting, Diarrhea, Dehydration, and Kidney-Medicine Review be reviewed?
Which interaction, storage, or side-effect details should be confirmed with a pharmacist?
What symptoms would make a medicine concern urgent instead of routine?
precautions: Do not keep relying on home monitoring if fluid loss is continuing and urine output is dropping.
Do not guess which chronic medicines to stop or restart without pharmacist or clinician review.
Repeated vomiting, confusion, fainting, reduced urine, severe weakness, or worsening illness needs prompt medical review.
The local library does not prescribe, approve, or calculate medicine doses.
Do not double a missed dose unless a licensed clinician or pharmacist instructed it.

records.title: Repeat Blood Pressure, Technique, and Headache Review
records.summary: Focuses on repeat blood-pressure checks by combining seated rest, cuff technique, headache or dizziness symptoms, baseline comparison, and when a high reading should move beyond home monitoring.
records.safetyNotes: Very high repeated readings with neurologic, chest, breathing, or fainting symptoms need urgent real-world care.
aliases: Repeat Blood Pressure, Technique, and Headache Review
whatToTrack: Two repeat readings after seated rest, cuff position, cuff size, arm level, and whether the device is working normally.
Headache, dizziness, vision change, weakness, chest symptoms, breathing trouble, and comparison with usual baseline.
Recent caffeine, pain, stress, missed medicines, activity, and whether readings stay elevated.
The exact reading, the time it was taken, the device used, and whether it was repeated correctly.
Symptoms present at the same time, recent food, caffeine, activity, medicines, or illness.
careQuestions: Which repeat-reading pattern is reasonable to track and which pattern needs same-day or urgent review?

What makes this reading more or less concerning in context?
What repeat-check method or follow-up question should be used for Repeat Blood Pressure, Technique, and Headache Review?
What urgent symptoms would change this from monitoring to immediate care?
precautions: Do not rely on one rushed home reading when symptoms are active and technique may be off.
Do not ignore repeat high readings when headache, dizziness, weakness, chest discomfort, or breathlessness are present.
Very high repeated readings with neurologic, chest, breathing, or fainting symptoms need urgent real-world care.
One reading should be interpreted with symptoms, technique, and trend, not alone.
Urgent symptoms override routine tracking or lifestyle review.
